title: ECOGEO 'Omics Training: 4.4 Phylogenetics
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"5ieje","text":"Provides a short introduction to phylogenetics and how to build a phylogenetic tree from concatenated ribosomal marker proteins. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"9eqkl","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"bdqoi","text":"Open this protocol inside the virtual machine (details in 'Start Instructions') for easy copy, paste of commands into the command line terminal window. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{}}
before_start: {"blocks":[{"key":"f3ovu","text":"Before starting, please visit the ECOGEO website for more information on this \"Introduction to Environmental 'Omics\" training series. The site contains a pre-packaged virtual machine that can be downloaded and used to run all of the protocols in this protocols.io collection. In addition to the VM, the website contains video and presentations from our initial \"Intro to Env 'Omics\" workshop held at the Univ. of Hawai'i at Manoa on 25-26 Jul 2016.\n\nPlease email ‘ecogeo-join@earthcube.org’ to join the ECOGEO listserv for future updates.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: Demo for identifying marker genes using RiboDB . Marker genes from reference genomes Hug et al (2016). 16 Ribosomal proteins: L2, L3, L4, L5, L6, L14, L15, L16, L18, L22, L24, S3, S8, S10, S17, S19 Flavobacteria and Prochlorococcaceae → RpL14 (uL14), RpL22 (uL22), RpS8 (bS8), RpS18 (uS18) https://ribodb.univ-lyon1.fr/ribodb/ribodb-in.cgi Paste in Flavobacteriia (yes, with 2 i's) $ cd /home/c-debi/ecogeo/secrets/secretphylodir Secret directory with pre-computed files. To access, open a new terminal window and cd.
Move compressed file to /home/c-debi/ecogeo/phylogenetics $ mv R-PROTS.tar.gz /home/c-debi/ecogeo/phylogenetics
Decompress file: Contains 4 folders – bS18, uL14, uL22, uS8 Each contains a number of files – 3 nucleotide and 3 protein FASTA files $ tar zxvf R-PROTS.tar.gz

Navigate to each folder and copy the *_prot.fst files up two directories to /home/ecogeo/phylogenetics Navigate to each folder and use: $ cp *_prot.fst /home/c-debi/ecogeo/phylogenetics $ cp *_prot.fst /home/c-debi/ecogeo/phylogenetics

Clean up header names & repeat: When renaming: Check that you get unique names! $ cut -f1 -d temp1_bS18 $ rm bS18_prot.fst $ mv temp1_bS18 bS18_prot.fst" description="" os_version="" os_name="" guid="A9F38F7E841B499A90E378A8ADBC3CF4" > $ cut -f1 -d "~" bS18_prot.fst | sed 's/\.//' | sed 's/|//' > temp1_bS18 $ rm bS18_prot.fst $ mv temp1_bS18 bS18_prot.fst
MUSCLE performs an alignment of the sequences and generates an aligned FASTA file output $ muscle -maxiters 8 -in bS18_prot.fst -out bS18_prot.aln
Trim alignments in Jalview . http://www.jalview.org/ $ Jalview
TrimAI - performs automated trimming for which various parameters can be set for columns to include or exclude $ trimal -in bS18_prot.aln -out bS18_prot.trimal.aln -automated1

For a reasonable number of sequences possible to use Galaxy . Follow steps in presentation for manipulating data in Galaxy. After you download the results, change the name to "ribomarkers.concat.fasta" Combine aligned files (specifically for Galaxy format): https://galaxyproject.org/ $ cat *mfa | cut -f1 -d

Construct a maximum likelihood tree with FastTree. Remove 5 sequences from ribomarkers.concat.fasta in Text Editor -candidatus_sulcia_muelleri -Flavobacterium_indicumGPTSA100-9+DSM17447 -Flavobacterium_psychrophilum -Riemerella_anatipestiferATCC11845+DSM15868 -Prochlorococcus_spMIT0801

Execute FastTree: If this command can not read ribomarkers.edit.concat.fasta, editing may have altered the file in the previous step. Copy the file from /secretphylodir, run command again, and confirm your newick file has data.

In Dendroscopy, “Enter Tree in Newick Format” requires copying and pasting the text version of ribomarkers.concat.newick in to the window Visualize tree in Dendroscope:
